summary: Graham recounts discovering a patent from his Viaweb days for ranking search results by bid times transactions, arguing this yields better relevance and maximizes revenue. He explains why Yahoo in 1998 wasn’t interested because advertisers were overpaying, and notes how the industry later embraced the approach. He also laments how legal edits made the patent prose less clear.
key_points: Revenue Loop ranks results by bid multiplied by transactions, aligning relevance with actual purchases.
This method avoids low-quality sites dominating results while still maximizing search engine revenue.
Yahoo in 1998 had no incentive to optimize ad value because advertisers overpaid, so the idea drew little interest.
The patent issued years later, highlighting how ideas can be ahead of market readiness.
Legal rewriting degraded the clarity of the original technical explanation.
money_quotes: But if you order results by bid multiplied by transactions, far from selling out, you're getting a better measure of relevance.
What could be a better sign that someone was satisfied with a search result than going to the site and buying something?
In 1998, if advertisers paid the maximum that traffic was worth to them, Yahoo's revenues would have decreased.
The main thing that struck me on reading it, actually, is that lawyers at some point messed up my nice clear writing.
Some clever person with a spell checker reduced one section to Zen-like incomprehensibility.
key_insight: An algorithm that ties search ranking to actual transactions can improve relevance and revenue, but market incentives and timing can keep even strong ideas from being valued when they first appear.
